title: Non-abelian Rees Construction and Motives
speaker: Yves Andre
affiliation: Institut de Mathématiques de Jussieu - Paris Rive Gauche
abstract:
The classical Rees construction (of common use in commutative algebra and Hodge theory) interpolates between filtrations, viewed as Gm
-equivariant vector bundles on the affine line, and their associated gradings. Various non-abelian versions have been proposed, where Gm
 is replaced by a reductive group. We shall present  a Galois correspondence between prehomogeneous spaces and certain monodical categories, and apply it to monoidal categories of motives with concrete applications to algebraic cycles.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: Classical Rees construction and non-abelian questions
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the Rees construction for filtered vector spaces as a G_m-equivariant vector bundle on A^1 deforming filtered objects to graded objects, then asks what replaces the deformation space and the filtration description when G_m is replaced by a reductive, possibly non-abelian, group.

proof_stages.label: Equivariant bundles on fixed-point spherical varieties
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces quasi-homogeneous/prehomogeneous varieties, spherical varieties, and fixed-pointed affine varieties, and states that for fixed-pointed affine spherical G-varieties the tensor category of G-equivariant vector bundles admits an explicit description in terms of representations and attached filtrations.

proof_stages.label: O'Sullivan deformation space for tensor categories
proof_stages.summary: Using O'Sullivan's theorem for even rigid symmetric tensor categories, a maximal tensor ideal quotient with tensor section is used to construct an algebra O(X), giving a fixed-point affine G-variety X such that the original category is equivalent to G-equivariant vector bundles on X.

proof_stages.label: Finite quasi-homogeneous reconstruction under P1-P3
proof_stages.summary: Additional hypotheses—existence of a fiber functor/rational point, tensor generation by one object, and finite generation of morphisms—are shown to characterize the categories coming from fixed-pointed affine quasi-homogeneous G-varieties, yielding an anti-equivalence, an explicit formula for O(X), and functorial criteria.

proof_stages.label: Motivic obstruction to standard conjecture D
proof_stages.summary: For categories of pure motives modulo homological equivalence, under the needed sign/evenness assumptions, the non-abelian Rees construction produces a numerical motivic group G and a deformation space X, with the standard conjecture D equivalent to X being a point and intermediate adequate equivalences corresponding to G-orbits in X.

proof_stages.label: Luna-theoretic point criterion and first abelian-variety application
proof_stages.summary: A first criterion uses an isotypic tensor generator and a reductive subgroup of the stabilizer, together with Luna's theorem excluding nontrivial affine embeddings of G/H, to force the fixed-pointed quasi-homogeneous space X to be a point, giving an application to certain abelian varieties over finite fields via Tate-type endomorphism control.

proof_stages.label: Diagonalizable-group criterion with involution
proof_stages.summary: A second criterion assumes that after scalar extension the motivic group is diagonalizable and an involution acts by -1 on its character group; the explicit formula for O(X) then turns the relevant monoid of characters into a group, forcing X to be homogeneous and hence a point.

proof_stages.label: CM finite-field abelian varieties and broader motivic scope
proof_stages.summary: The diagonalizable criterion is applied to abelian varieties over algebraic closures of finite fields using a CM Galois extension and a compatible p-adic place, giving a new proof of numerical equals homological equivalence and indicating extensions to other motivic categories with the same compactness property.

examples.name: Vector bundles on the projective line
examples.note: The category of vector bundles on P^1 illustrates O'Sullivan's construction and shows that the deformation space can be A^2 rather than A^1 and need not be quasi-homogeneous, motivating the added hypotheses used next.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker takes the category of vector bundles on P1 and identifies its quotient, the group Gm, the deformation space as A2, and the corresponding equivariant-vector-bundle category.

passes.reason: The homogeneous, very regular P^1 vector-bundle case is used to show the bare construction can give A^2 and need not be quasi-homogeneous.
Vector bundles on the genus-zero projective line form a highly split base case, and the example is used to show the general deformation space can be affine plane rather than quasi-homogeneous.

Vector bundles on P^1 form a highly regular minimal genus case where all bundles split, and the example is used to show the general construction need not give the original/quasi-homogeneous deformation behavior.
Vector bundles on P^1 form a highly regular split category, and here they show the general construction can give A^2 and fail quasi-homogeneity/embedding properties.
Vector bundles on P^1 form the genus-zero highly regular splitting case, used here to show the deformation space need not be quasi-homogeneous or give an embedding without extra hypotheses.
Vector bundles on P^1 form a highly regular homogeneous example, used here to show the naive deformation space need not be quasi-homogeneous or embed as in the classical Rees case.
Vector bundles on P^1 are a genus-zero/splitting boundary case, and here they are used to show that the deformation space need not retain the desired quasi-homogeneous/Rees-like properties.

P^1 is the minimal-genus homogeneous curve with completely split vector bundles, and the example warns that the unrestricted construction need not have the classical quasi-homogeneous/embedding behavior.
The projective line is a maximally homogeneous genus-zero curve with split vector bundles, and it is used here to show the deformation space need not be quasi-homogeneous or Rees-like.
Used to show the general deformation can be affine plane and not quasi-homogeneous; over P^1 vector bundles split and the genus-zero base is highly regular.
Used to show the general Rees/O’Sullivan deformation can fail to be quasi-homogeneous or embedding-like; P^1 is the genus-zero homogeneous curve with unusually split vector bundles.
P^1 is the genus-zero homogeneous curve with all vector bundles splitting, and it is used to show the deformation space need not have the desired quasi-homogeneous/Rees-line behavior.

examples.name: Representations of the additive group and SL2
examples.note: The category of representations of G_a is identified, via a Jacobson–Morozov-type description, with SL2-equivariant vector bundles on A^2, illustrating the anti-equivalence/reconstruction theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the construction to representations of the additive group, identifying the associated reductive group as SL2, the open orbit, stabilizer, and equivariant-bundle description.

passes.reason: The Rep(G_a)/SL2 example is a standard Jacobson-Morozov illustration, not a sporadic or adversarial case.
The additive-group/SL2 representation example is the standard Jacobson-Morozov rank-one illustration, not a sporadic, extremal, or refuting case.
Representations of G_a with the associated SL2 are a standard Jacobson–Morozov illustration of the construction, not a sporadic or adversarial case.
Rep(G_a) and its relation to SL2 are used as a standard illustrative Jacobson–monodromy example, not to refute a claim.
The Rep(G_a)/SL2 example is the standard Jacobson--Morozov illustration of the construction, not a failure case or sporadic object.
The additive-group/SL2 representation example is an ordinary Jacobson-Morozov illustration rather than an adversarial case.
The Rep(Ga)/SL2 example is used as a standard illustrative instance of the construction via Jacobson–Morozov, not to refute a claim.
Rep(Ga) is the one-parameter unipotent base case related to SL2 by Jacobson-Morozov, used here as a positive illustrative example.
Representations of the additive group form the minimal unipotent/nilpotent case paired with SL2 by Jacobson--Morozov, and the example is presented as a positive illustration.
Used as a positive Jacobson–Morozov illustration; the additive group is the minimal one-dimensional unipotent case paired with rank-one SL2.
A positive Jacobson–Morozov-style illustration relating Rep(G_a) to SL2-equivariant bundles, not used to refute a claim.
Rep(Ga) with its SL2 semisimplification is the rank-one unipotent/Jacobson-Morozov base case used as a positive illustration.

examples.name: Fibonacci tensor category
examples.note: A tensor category generated by two rank-one objects with morphisms controlled by a golden-ratio inequality yields an inverse limit of toric surfaces, showing why the finite-generation condition P3 is needed for a finite-dimensional deformation space.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker briefly describes a 'Fibonacci category' and states the resulting x as a limit of toric surfaces, without working through the construction or computation.

passes.reason: The golden-ratio Fibonacci category is used to show that dropping the finite-generation condition can make the deformation space an infinite inverse limit.
The Fibonacci category is built around the golden-ratio limiting boundary and is used to show that the finite-generation condition is needed for finite-dimensional deformation spaces.
The speaker’s Fibonacci/golden-ratio tensor category is used to show that dropping the finite-generation condition can make the deformation space an infinite tower rather than finite-dimensional.
The Fibonacci/golden-ratio tensor category is a boundary-type non-finite-generation example used to show condition P3 is necessary for finite-dimensional x.
The Fibonacci-style tensor category is used to show that dropping the finite-generation condition can make the deformation space an infinite toric inverse limit.
The Fibonacci tensor category is used to show that dropping the finite-generation condition can make the deformation space an infinite inverse limit.
The golden-ratio/Fibonacci category is a limiting toric-tower example used to show that dropping the finite-generation condition can make the deformation space non-finite-dimensional.
The golden-ratio/Fibonacci tensor category is a limiting irrational-boundary example used to show the finite-generation condition is necessary for finite-dimensional x.
The Fibonacci/golden-ratio slope is a boundary irrational case producing an infinite limiting tower, used to show the finite-generation hypothesis is necessary.
Used to show dropping the finite-generation condition can give an infinite inverse-limit toric deformation space; its golden-ratio cutoff is a boundary construction.
Used to show the finite-generation condition is necessary, since the golden-ratio/Fibonacci construction gives a limiting inverse tower rather than a finite-dimensional space.
The golden-ratio Fibonacci category is a boundary/pro-toric limit example used to show the finite-generation condition is necessary.

examples.name: Compactification of an abelian scheme over a curve
examples.note: This motivic test case illustrates how the obstruction space X can be computed and how its being a point corresponds to the standard conjecture D in concrete geometric situations.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker applies the motive/Rees construction to a compactified abelian scheme over a curve, discusses bypassing the sign issue, and describes computing the deformation space/obstruction and its possible outcomes.

passes.reason: A compactified abelian scheme over a curve is used as an ordinary motivic application for computing the obstruction, not to refute a claim.
A compactification of an abelian scheme over a curve is treated as a standard application class for computing the obstruction, not as a failure case or classification exception.
A compactification of an abelian scheme over a curve is used as a motives application where the obstruction can be computed, not to refute a claim.
A compactified abelian scheme over a curve is used as a motivating application where the obstruction can be computed, not as a disproof.
The compactification of an abelian scheme over a curve is a systematic motivic test case for computing the obstruction, not presented as a counterexample.
A compactified abelian scheme over a curve is used as a motivic application/illustration, not to refute a claim, and is not structurally sporadic or extremal here.
A compactified abelian scheme over a curve is used as an application/test case for computing the motivic deformation obstruction, with no structural sporadic or extremal feature invoked.
A compactification of an abelian scheme over a curve is used as a standard motivic test case rather than as a failure example or sporadic object.
The compactified abelian scheme over a curve is used as a motives application and computation test case, not as a sporadic, degenerate, extremal, or maximally symmetric object.
Used as a motives application/test case rather than a disproof; such compactified abelian schemes form a standard non-sporadic family.
An application computing the motivic deformation/obstruction for a standard abelian fibration over a curve, not deployed as a failure example.
A compactified abelian scheme over a curve is a systematic abelian-fibered motive example used as a computable application, not as a refutation.
